Real Analysis

The Cauchy Criterion

The Cauchy Criterion

Cauchy Sequences

So far, showing a sequence converges has always required knowing the limit in advance — the definition of xn→xx_n \to xxn​→x explicitly refers to xxx. The Cauchy criterion, developed in this lecture, will give a way to certify convergence using only the terms of the sequence themselves, with no candidate limit required.

Cauchy Sequence

A sequence X=(xn)X = (x_n)X=(xn​) is Cauchy if

∀ ϵ>0, ∃ hϵ∈N such that ∀ m,n≥hϵ,∣xn−xm∣<ϵ.\forall\, \epsilon > 0,\ \exists\, h_\epsilon \in \mathbb{N} \text{ such that } \forall\, m,n \ge h_\epsilon,\quad |x_n - x_m| < \epsilon.∀ϵ>0, ∃hϵ​∈N such that ∀m,n≥hϵ​,∣xn​−xm​∣<ϵ.
Reading the definition

Informally: past some index hϵh_\epsilonhϵ​, any two terms of the sequence — not just consecutive ones — are within ϵ\epsilonϵ of each other. This is a stronger-looking condition than “consecutive terms get close,” since it must hold for every pair m,n≥hϵm,n \ge h_\epsilonm,n≥hϵ​ simultaneously.

Example: (1/n)(1/n)(1/n) Is Cauchy

xn=1/nx_n = 1/nxn​=1/n Is Cauchy

We want ∣1n−1m∣<ϵ\left|\tfrac1n - \tfrac1m\right| < \epsilon​n1​−m1​​<ϵ. By the triangle inequality,

∣1n−1m∣≤1n+1m.\left|\frac1n - \frac1m\right| \le \frac1n + \frac1m.​n1​−m1​​≤n1​+m1​.

It suffices to make each term less than ϵ/2\epsilon/2ϵ/2: if n,m>2/ϵn, m > 2/\epsilonn,m>2/ϵ, then 1n<ϵ/2\tfrac1n < \epsilon/2n1​<ϵ/2 and 1m<ϵ/2\tfrac1m < \epsilon/2m1​<ϵ/2, so their sum is less than ϵ\epsilonϵ. By the Archimedean property, some hϵ>2/ϵh_\epsilon > 2/\epsilonhϵ​>2/ϵ exists; taking any m,n≥hϵm,n \ge h_\epsilonm,n≥hϵ​ then gives ∣1n−1m∣<ϵ\left|\tfrac1n-\tfrac1m\right| < \epsilon​n1​−m1​​<ϵ. Hence (1/n)(1/n)(1/n) is Cauchy.

Negation: ((−1)n)((-1)^n)((−1)n) Is Not Cauchy

Negating the Cauchy condition follows the same pattern as negating convergence in Lecture 9: flip the quantifiers, and produce a single “bad” pair of indices for every threshold.

Non-Cauchy (Negation)

X=(xn)X = (x_n)X=(xn​) fails to be Cauchy if

∃ ϵ0>0 such that ∀ k∈N, ∃ mk,nk≥k with ∣xmk−xnk∣≥ϵ0.\exists\, \epsilon_0 > 0 \text{ such that } \forall\, k \in \mathbb{N},\ \exists\, m_k, n_k \ge k \text{ with } |x_{m_k} - x_{n_k}| \ge \epsilon_0.∃ϵ0​>0 such that ∀k∈N, ∃mk​,nk​≥k with ∣xmk​​−xnk​​∣≥ϵ0​.
xn=(−1)nx_n = (-1)^nxn​=(−1)n Is Not Cauchy

Note that ∣(−1)n−(−1)n+1∣=2|(-1)^n - (-1)^{n+1}| = 2∣(−1)n−(−1)n+1∣=2 for every nnn, so any ϵ0<2\epsilon_0 < 2ϵ0​<2 will witness non-Cauchyness. Take ϵ0=1\epsilon_0 = 1ϵ0​=1. For any k∈Nk \in \mathbb{N}k∈N, let nk=kn_k = knk​=k and mk=k+1m_k = k+1mk​=k+1; both satisfy nk,mk≥kn_k, m_k \ge knk​,mk​≥k, and

∣xmk−xnk∣=∣(−1)k+1−(−1)k∣=2>1=ϵ0.|x_{m_k} - x_{n_k}| = |(-1)^{k+1} - (-1)^k| = 2 > 1 = \epsilon_0.∣xmk​​−xnk​​∣=∣(−1)k+1−(−1)k∣=2>1=ϵ0​.

Hence (−1)n(-1)^n(−1)n is not Cauchy.

The Cauchy Convergence Criterion

We now come to the main result: being Cauchy is not merely necessary for convergence but equivalent to it. This is the payoff promised above — a way to certify convergence purely from the terms of the sequence.

Cauchy Convergence Criterion

A sequence (xn)(x_n)(xn​) converges if and only if it is Cauchy.

The forward direction is a routine application of the triangle inequality, splitting ϵ\epsilonϵ into halves exactly as in the Addition Law proof (Lecture 7).

(⇒\Rightarrow⇒) Convergent Implies Cauchy

Suppose xn→xx_n \to xxn​→x. Given ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0, choose nϵn_\epsilonnϵ​ such that ∣xn−x∣<ϵ/2|x_n - x| < \epsilon/2∣xn​−x∣<ϵ/2 for all n≥nϵn \ge n_\epsilonn≥nϵ​. Take hϵ=nϵh_\epsilon = n_\epsilonhϵ​=nϵ​. Then for all m,n≥hϵm, n \ge h_\epsilonm,n≥hϵ​, the triangle inequality gives

∣xn−xm∣=∣(xn−x)+(x−xm)∣≤∣xn−x∣+∣xm−x∣<ϵ2+ϵ2=ϵ.|x_n - x_m| = |(x_n - x) + (x - x_m)| \le |x_n - x| + |x_m - x| < \frac{\epsilon}{2} + \frac{\epsilon}{2} = \epsilon.∣xn​−xm​∣=∣(xn​−x)+(x−xm​)∣≤∣xn​−x∣+∣xm​−x∣<2ϵ​+2ϵ​=ϵ.

Hence (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is Cauchy.

The converse is the harder direction, and it is where the machinery from earlier lectures pays off: boundedness (Lecture 7), Bolzano–Weierstrass (Lecture 10), and the triangle inequality all combine in a three-step argument.

(⇐\Leftarrow⇐) Cauchy Implies Convergent

Step 1: (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is bounded. Since (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is Cauchy, take ϵ=1\epsilon = 1ϵ=1: there exists h1h_1h1​ such that ∣xn−xm∣<1|x_n - x_m| < 1∣xn​−xm​∣<1 for all m,n≥h1m,n \ge h_1m,n≥h1​. In particular, fixing m=h1m = h_1m=h1​, we get ∣xn−xh1∣<1|x_n - x_{h_1}| < 1∣xn​−xh1​​∣<1 for all n≥h1n \ge h_1n≥h1​. By the triangle inequality,

∣xn∣≤∣xn−xh1∣+∣xh1∣<1+∣xh1∣for all n≥h1.|x_n| \le |x_n - x_{h_1}| + |x_{h_1}| < 1 + |x_{h_1}| \qquad \text{for all } n \ge h_1.∣xn​∣≤∣xn​−xh1​​∣+∣xh1​​∣<1+∣xh1​​∣for all n≥h1​.

Setting M:=max⁡{∣x1∣,…,∣xh1−1∣, 1+∣xh1∣}M := \max\{|x_1|, \dots, |x_{h_1-1}|,\ 1+|x_{h_1}|\}M:=max{∣x1​∣,…,∣xh1​−1​∣, 1+∣xh1​​∣} gives ∣xn∣≤M|x_n| \le M∣xn​∣≤M for all n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N — exactly the same “bound the tail, then take a max over the finite start” argument used to show convergent sequences are bounded in Lecture 7.

Step 2: Extract a convergent subsequence. Since (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is bounded, the Bolzano–Weierstrass theorem (Lecture 10) guarantees a convergent subsequence (xnk)(x_{n_k})(xnk​​). Let L=lim⁡k→∞xnkL = \lim_{k\to\infty} x_{n_k}L=limk→∞​xnk​​.

Step 3: Show the whole sequence converges to LLL. Let ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0. Since (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is Cauchy, there exists hϵ/2h_{\epsilon/2}hϵ/2​ such that ∣xn−xm∣<ϵ/2|x_n - x_m| < \epsilon/2∣xn​−xm​∣<ϵ/2 for all m,n≥hϵ/2m,n \ge h_{\epsilon/2}m,n≥hϵ/2​. Since xnk→Lx_{n_k} \to Lxnk​​→L, there exists kϵ/2k_{\epsilon/2}kϵ/2​ such that ∣xnk−L∣<ϵ/2|x_{n_k} - L| < \epsilon/2∣xnk​​−L∣<ϵ/2 for all k≥kϵ/2k \ge k_{\epsilon/2}k≥kϵ/2​. Choose kkk large enough that both k≥kϵ/2k \ge k_{\epsilon/2}k≥kϵ/2​ and nk≥hϵ/2n_k \ge h_{\epsilon/2}nk​≥hϵ/2​ (possible since (nk)(n_k)(nk​) is strictly increasing and unbounded). Then for any n≥hϵ/2n \ge h_{\epsilon/2}n≥hϵ/2​, the triangle inequality gives

∣xn−L∣≤∣xn−xnk∣⏟< ϵ/2 (Cauchy)+∣xnk−L∣⏟< ϵ/2 (subsequence)<ϵ.|x_n - L| \le \underbrace{|x_n - x_{n_k}|}_{<\, \epsilon/2 \text{ (Cauchy)}} + \underbrace{|x_{n_k} - L|}_{<\, \epsilon/2 \text{ (subsequence)}} < \epsilon.∣xn​−L∣≤<ϵ/2 (Cauchy)∣xn​−xnk​​∣​​+<ϵ/2 (subsequence)∣xnk​​−L∣​​<ϵ.

Hence xn→Lx_n \to Lxn​→L.

Why this criterion is powerful

Every earlier convergence proof (Lectures 7–8) required guessing the limit LLL first, then verifying ∣xn−L∣<ϵ|x_n - L| < \epsilon∣xn​−L∣<ϵ. The Cauchy criterion sidesteps this entirely — it only inspects how the terms of the sequence relate to each other. This is especially valuable for proving divergence, since showing a sequence is not Cauchy requires no knowledge of a limit at all, as the next example shows.

Application: The Harmonic Series Diverges

xn=1+12+13+⋯+1nx_n = 1 + \tfrac12 + \tfrac13 + \cdots + \tfrac1nxn​=1+21​+31​+⋯+n1​ Diverges

We show (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is not Cauchy. For n>mn > mn>m,

xn−xm=1m+1+1m+2+⋯+1n.x_n - x_m = \frac{1}{m+1} + \frac{1}{m+2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{n}.xn​−xm​=m+11​+m+21​+⋯+n1​.

Take n=2mn = 2mn=2m. This difference has exactly mmm terms, each at least 12m\tfrac{1}{2m}2m1​ (since every denominator is at most 2m2m2m), so

x2m−xm≥12m+⋯+12m⏟m terms=m⋅12m=12.x_{2m} - x_m \ge \underbrace{\frac{1}{2m} + \cdots + \frac{1}{2m}}_{m \text{ terms}} = m \cdot \frac{1}{2m} = \frac12.x2m​−xm​≥m terms2m1​+⋯+2m1​​​=m⋅2m1​=21​.

So with ϵ0=1/2\epsilon_0 = 1/2ϵ0​=1/2, set nk=2kn_k = 2knk​=2k and mk=km_k = kmk​=k: both are ≥k\ge k≥k, and ∣xnk−xmk∣≥1/2=ϵ0|x_{n_k} - x_{m_k}| \ge 1/2 = \epsilon_0∣xnk​​−xmk​​∣≥1/2=ϵ0​ for every kkk. Hence (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is not Cauchy, and by the Cauchy Convergence Criterion, it does not converge — the harmonic series diverges.

Contractive Sequences

The Cauchy criterion is often hard to verify directly, since it requires controlling ∣xn−xm∣|x_n - x_m|∣xn​−xm​∣ for arbitrary pairs m,nm,nm,n. Contractive sequences are a special class where only consecutive differences need to be controlled, and where those differences shrink geometrically — making the Cauchy condition automatic.

Contractive Sequence

A sequence (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is contractive if there exists a constant ccc with 0<c<10 < c < 10<c<1 such that, for all sufficiently large nnn,

∣xn+2−xn+1∣≤c ∣xn+1−xn∣.|x_{n+2} - x_{n+1}| \le c\,|x_{n+1} - x_n|.∣xn+2​−xn+1​∣≤c∣xn+1​−xn​∣.
A Recursively Averaged Sequence

Let x1=1x_1 = 1x1​=1, x2=2x_2 = 2x2​=2, and xn=12(xn−1+xn−2)x_n = \tfrac12(x_{n-1} + x_{n-2})xn​=21​(xn−1​+xn−2​) for n≥3n \ge 3n≥3. Then

xn+2−xn+1=12(xn+1+xn)−xn+1=12(xn−xn+1),x_{n+2} - x_{n+1} = \frac12(x_{n+1}+x_n) - x_{n+1} = \frac12(x_n - x_{n+1}),xn+2​−xn+1​=21​(xn+1​+xn​)−xn+1​=21​(xn​−xn+1​),

so

∣xn+2−xn+1∣=12∣xn+1−xn∣.|x_{n+2} - x_{n+1}| = \frac12 |x_{n+1} - x_n|.∣xn+2​−xn+1​∣=21​∣xn+1​−xn​∣.

This is exactly the contractive condition with c=1/2c = 1/2c=1/2.

Every Contractive Sequence Is Cauchy (Hence Convergent)

If (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is contractive with constant c∈(0,1)c \in (0,1)c∈(0,1), then (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is Cauchy, and therefore convergent by the Cauchy Convergence Criterion.

The proof has two ingredients: first, iterating the contractive inequality shows consecutive differences decay geometrically; second, summing a geometric series (via the finite geometric sum formula) bounds the distance between any two terms, however far apart.

Proof

Geometric decay of consecutive differences. Iterating the contractive inequality,

∣xn+2−xn+1∣≤c ∣xn+1−xn∣≤c2 ∣xn−xn−1∣≤⋯≤cn−1∣x2−x1∣.|x_{n+2}-x_{n+1}| \le c\,|x_{n+1}-x_n| \le c^2\,|x_n - x_{n-1}| \le \cdots \le c^{n-1}|x_2-x_1|.∣xn+2​−xn+1​∣≤c∣xn+1​−xn​∣≤c2∣xn​−xn−1​∣≤⋯≤cn−1∣x2​−x1​∣.

Bounding ∣xn−xm∣|x_n - x_m|∣xn​−xm​∣ for n>mn > mn>m. Chaining the triangle inequality across consecutive terms,

∣xn−xm∣≤∣xn−xn−1∣+∣xn−1−xn−2∣+⋯+∣xm+1−xm∣≤cn−2∣x2−x1∣+⋯+cm−1∣x2−x1∣.|x_n - x_m| \le |x_n - x_{n-1}| + |x_{n-1}-x_{n-2}| + \cdots + |x_{m+1}-x_m| \le c^{n-2}|x_2-x_1| + \cdots + c^{m-1}|x_2-x_1|.∣xn​−xm​∣≤∣xn​−xn−1​∣+∣xn−1​−xn−2​∣+⋯+∣xm+1​−xm​∣≤cn−2∣x2​−x1​∣+⋯+cm−1∣x2​−x1​∣.

Factor out cm−1∣x2−x1∣c^{m-1}|x_2-x_1|cm−1∣x2​−x1​∣:

∣xn−xm∣≤cm−1∣x2−x1∣(1+c+c2+⋯+cn−m−1).|x_n - x_m| \le c^{m-1}|x_2-x_1|\left(1 + c + c^2 + \cdots + c^{n-m-1}\right).∣xn​−xm​∣≤cm−1∣x2​−x1​∣(1+c+c2+⋯+cn−m−1).

Using the finite geometric sum formula 1+a+⋯+ak=1−ak+11−a1 + a + \cdots + a^k = \dfrac{1-a^{k+1}}{1-a}1+a+⋯+ak=1−a1−ak+1​ with a=ca = ca=c,

∣xn−xm∣≤cm−1∣x2−x1∣⋅1−cn−m1−c=∣x2−x1∣1−c(cm−1−cn−1)≤∣x2−x1∣1−c cm−1,|x_n-x_m| \le c^{m-1}|x_2-x_1| \cdot \frac{1-c^{n-m}}{1-c} = \frac{|x_2-x_1|}{1-c}\bigl(c^{m-1}-c^{n-1}\bigr) \le \frac{|x_2-x_1|}{1-c}\,c^{m-1},∣xn​−xm​∣≤cm−1∣x2​−x1​∣⋅1−c1−cn−m​=1−c∣x2​−x1​∣​(cm−1−cn−1)≤1−c∣x2​−x1​∣​cm−1,

since 0<c<10 < c < 10<c<1 makes cn−1>0c^{n-1} > 0cn−1>0. Because 0<c<10 < c < 10<c<1, cm−1→0c^{m-1} \to 0cm−1→0 as m→∞m \to \inftym→∞ (this is the decaying geometric sequence result from Lecture 9). So given ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0, choose hϵh_\epsilonhϵ​ large enough that cm−1<(1−c)ϵ∣x2−x1∣c^{m-1} < \dfrac{(1-c)\epsilon}{|x_2-x_1|}cm−1<∣x2​−x1​∣(1−c)ϵ​ for all m≥hϵm \ge h_\epsilonm≥hϵ​ (possible whenever x1≠x2x_1 \ne x_2x1​=x2​; if x1=x2x_1=x_2x1​=x2​ the sequence is eventually constant and trivially Cauchy). Then ∣xn−xm∣<ϵ|x_n - x_m| < \epsilon∣xn​−xm​∣<ϵ for all n>m≥hϵn > m \ge h_\epsilonn>m≥hϵ​, so (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is Cauchy.

Why contractive sequences are easier to handle

This theorem converts a hard-to-check global condition (Cauchy: control ∣xn−xm∣|x_n-x_m|∣xn​−xm​∣ for all pairs) into an easy-to-check local condition (contractive: control only consecutive differences). Verifying contractiveness is often a one-line computation, as the next example shows, while directly verifying the Cauchy condition for the same sequence would be far more work.

Example: A Recursive Square-Root Sequence

x1=1, xn+1=xn+2x_1 = 1,\ x_{n+1} = \sqrt{x_n+2}x1​=1, xn+1​=xn​+2​

We show this sequence is contractive, using the Conjugate Trick from Lecture 6. For consecutive differences,

∣xn+2−xn+1∣=∣xn+1+2−xn+2∣=∣(xn+1+2)−(xn+2)∣xn+1+2+xn+2=∣xn+1−xn∣xn+1+2+xn+2.|x_{n+2}-x_{n+1}| = \left|\sqrt{x_{n+1}+2}-\sqrt{x_n+2}\right| = \frac{|(x_{n+1}+2)-(x_n+2)|}{\sqrt{x_{n+1}+2}+\sqrt{x_n+2}} = \frac{|x_{n+1}-x_n|}{\sqrt{x_{n+1}+2}+\sqrt{x_n+2}}.∣xn+2​−xn+1​∣=​xn+1​+2​−xn​+2​​=xn+1​+2​+xn​+2​∣(xn+1​+2)−(xn​+2)∣​=xn+1​+2​+xn​+2​∣xn+1​−xn​∣​.

Since xn≥0x_n \ge 0xn​≥0 for all nnn (an easy induction, as xn+1=xn+2≥2>0x_{n+1} = \sqrt{x_n+2} \ge \sqrt{2} > 0xn+1​=xn​+2​≥2​>0), each term in the denominator satisfies xk+2≥2\sqrt{x_k+2} \ge \sqrt2xk​+2​≥2​, so

∣xn+2−xn+1∣≤122 ∣xn+1−xn∣.|x_{n+2}-x_{n+1}| \le \frac{1}{2\sqrt2}\,|x_{n+1}-x_n|.∣xn+2​−xn+1​∣≤22​1​∣xn+1​−xn​∣.

This is the contractive condition with c=122∈(0,1)c = \tfrac{1}{2\sqrt2} \in (0,1)c=22​1​∈(0,1). By the theorem above, (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is Cauchy, hence convergent.
